policy_statement: NAP target: reduce the number of people affected by floods in risk areas identified in Flood Risk Management Plans by 25% by 2030 (compared with previous PGRI cycle).
mechanism_description: Flood Risk Management Plans aim to reduce how many people in identified risk areas are affected by flooding by lowering flood exposure and vulnerability.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → risk planning + protective/avoidance measures → ↓ exposure & ↓ vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts (damages, disruption, casualties)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Governments develop and implement Flood Risk Management Plans (mapping risk, setting targets, prioritizing measures, coordinating agencies) that guide land-use controls, defenses, warnings, and preparedness in identified flood-prone areas.  
2) Mediating physical/social process: Reduced contact between floodwaters and people/assets via (a) keeping development out of high-risk zones, (b) increasing protection/retention/drainage capacity, and (c) improving warning/evacuation and preparedness to lessen harm when flooding occurs.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Fewer people affected by floods and lower flood-related losses under current and future (climate-driven) flood regimes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing people and assets exposed/vulnerable to flooding (thereby lowering flood damages and disruption) as flood hazard frequency/intensity increases with climate change.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing a climate hazard (flooding) to reduce exposure/vulnerability and thus reduce impacts.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The core objective is impact reduction from floods, not emissions reduction (Mitigation) or primarily lowering resource inputs (Resource Efficiency). While some measures may be nature-based, the mechanism is not inherently ecosystem enhancement; it is hazard-risk planning and exposure management, so the overarching category remains Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a plan relies mainly on wetland restoration/floodplain reconnection, it can have a strong Nature-Based Solutions component, but the dominant effect is still reducing flood impacts; mitigation co-benefits (e.g., reduced pumping energy, avoided reconstruction emissions) are secondary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using “flood risk management plans → flood exposure reduction” is fundamentally aimed at lowering exposure/vulnerability to flooding, which is adaptation by definition.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the plan is “grey infrastructure,” “nature-based,” “early warning,” or “managed retreat” is policy-evidence dependent and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Plans that are predominantly ecosystem-restoration-based could be tagged secondarily as Nature-Based Solutions, and plans dominated by land-use/managed retreat versus structural defenses differ in subtype, but the primary category remains Adaptation because the dominant outcome is reduced flood impacts.
key_indicators: “NAP target”; “reduce the number of people affected by floods”; “risk areas identified in the Flood Risk Management Plans”; “25%”; “by 2030”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be read as part of a broader planning framework (Flood Risk Management Plans), but the text itself is an outcome metric/goal with a percentage reduction and deadline, so it fits target-setting rather than planning-strategy or infrastructure-investment.
